Last night, completed Bust 4 with a 1/2 tab of Vitamin L.  Have not had a legit attack since before Bust 3 but still experience soreness and occasional shadows, so I thought one more Bust would be appropriate.
On the Bust itself:
- significant body discomfort, experienced mostly as jaw and neck tension combined with agitation.  This was not a serious problem, but it was not especially pleasant either.  I paced quite a bit
- lots of visual changes in the form of shifting patterns and breathing floors, walls, etc
All in all,
though I have certainly had some good times, I am
ready for a break from The Vitamin L recreational experience.
medicinal effects:
- during the Bust, experienced occasional pressure in CH area.  This was not significant enough to qualify as a shadow.
That's about it!
The night before last, I went out to a nice dinner with my SO and another couple we've had planned for months.  We had a wine pairing with our dinner, so I had seven different wines, mostly reds, and a sweet wine with dessert.  I mention it because red wine is an almost guaranteed trigger for me as is anything overly sweet.  The meal passed without even a shadow!  I even had a couple martinis at cocktail hour before dinner.  I had an injection of Imitrex back in my room in case things broke bad, but did not have to think about it.
normally, I wouldn't dream of drinking so much as a beer during or around a CH cycle but with the success of the Vitamin L and D, I was able to enjoy myself.  It's a different world.  We've spent the past month wondering if we should cancel this dinner because of my cycle; quite empowering to go through with it like this.
*****
if anybody is reading this looking for advice:
-start the MM or L as soon as you can, and space them apart 5 days, per protocol.  I did 4 to avoid a Christmas Day Bust but I think 5 would have been better
-start the Vitamin D regime immediately. I eventually doubled my D to 20,000 ius a day but YMMV.  I'm a big guy.
-try aborting attacks with ice cold water and 5 hour energy drinks (these are far superior to any others, because you can bolt them down immediately).
-very strong ginger for shadows and neck massages at cluster spots, specifically, tendons on side of neck and under ear.  Strong coffee helped my shadows as well
-only take Imitrex if it's an emergency.  I have found I get significant increase in cluster attacks following Imitrex use and a marked increase in pain level
-keep a diary and be methodical.   When did your cycle start?  When does each attack start? How long does an attack last?  What did you do to stop it?  Use this to identify possible triggers and possible actions that help you.   It's also something to focus on and be constructive with when you're up in the middle of the night in excruciating pain
-find a doctor who will prescribe oxygen for you. It is not expensive out of pocket or through insurance.  Advocate for yourself on this or ask someone to help you advocate.

Towards the end of 2024 I was finding reduced efficacy of MM and RC seeds in a manner many here will be familiar with. Because of that I tried Ketamine for the first time and the results were promising. I have posted on various other topics during my experiments but thought I'd present a summary here of my experiences one year later.
Couple of caveats first - I am not a trained medical professional so none of this is medical advice, but through neccessity I have become quite knowledgable about medications and biology, I'm sure you know what I mean. I have based my dosages and methods on available research papers for Ketamine treatment of migraine. Unfortunately where I am Ketamine is very tightly controlled so I am sourcing it off the record, however I am confident in its purity - unlike many other meds Ketamine is presented as clear crystaline shards rather than any sort of powder, it's not something that is normally cut with bulking agents etc and it's so cheap that it doesn't require cutting.
A word on safety - please thoroughly research K if you plan on trying it, it is a dissociative anaesthetic and those words alone should hint at the potential dangers involved. Remember to also check for contra-indications although it appears to be pretty resiliant on that front. Also worht mentioning that long-term use can cause permanent inflammatory damage to the bowel and/or bladder. This aspect of K is poorly understood so always err on the side of taking less where possible. Please do your own research into this. Addiction potential is low but it can be compulsive so worth checking your behaviour on a regular basis.
My 2024 experiences with Ketamine coincided with me also trying other things, so I decided that 2025 would be Ketamine-only to see how successful it was. And one year later I'm pleased to report that I've had my clearest year in probably 15 years. Hopefully my suggested regime will help some others find similar success.
Many Ketamine migraine studies involve nasal sprays with 15mg doses taken on onset, repeated after 15 minutes if necessary. That was my starting point - 15mg insufflated ketamine at the onset of a cluster attack, repeated 2 or 3 more times as necessary during an attack . Most of the time this completely aborted it, and even during what I believe was probably a KIP 10 the numbing effect of 3 doses helped reduce the sensations almost entirely (yet the experience still felt like a KIP 10 without most of the pain,very hard to describe how it felt that way). This must be done in a safe environment (bedroom is perfect) as you will definitely need to lie down for more than one dose.
Aside from dosing during attacks, I also took a bedtime single dose of 20mg if I was developing shadows that evening. By the beginning of Feb 2025 I was no longer suffering attacks and simply taking a bedtime dose when shadows appeared.
That's pretty much it. One year on and if memory serves correctly I've not had a single attack since. I treat the fi

Hi Everyone,
I just wanted to share my story and my Miracle. I was diagnosed with CH 3 years ago and was subsequently labeled Refractory.  I was cycling into a Chronic state when I found Ketamine infusions. For 3 years I had experienced severe attacks 6-8x day for 2-3 hours each attack. Every treatment I tried and had any success with eventually stopped working. I was severely depressed with daily Anxiety and PTSD symptoms and had not been able to work or honestly function at all for 2 years. I went into the Ketamine clinic with absolutely no hope or belief that it would help me at all. It is hard to put into words all that I have experienced since my first infusion, but I can say this, it is an absolute Miracle for me and has completely stopped the CH attacks. Not only have I been in complete Remission, but also, I have had all symptoms of depression  and anxiety eliminated. The Ketamine clinic I use has been open since 2008 and my doctor reports better results using Ketamine on CH patients than Migraine or Treatment Resistant Headache patients. I was actively having a CH attack during my second Ketamine infusion and it not only aborted the attack but it also put me into immediate remission and I remain in remission today. I had a total of 7 higher dose Ketamine infusions lasting 2.5 hours each over a 2 week period. I now take Naltrexone 4.5 mg daily and Ketamine Lozenges 75mg daily and have a Ketamine booster IV when needed (which is about every 4-6 weeks at this time). I am going on 4 months and I can only say that I feel completely different than I did during the short remissions I experienced in the last 3 years. I am slowly coming back to life. I know that only those who suffer or have suffered with this unbelievably horrific disorder can understand. I know that what I have experienced is a true miracle for me and I know beyond a shadow of a doubt that I am alive today because of Ketamine. I hope this reaches someone who needs to hear this. I believe this could be a game changer for many CH patients. I am Grateful to the core of my being for every day I have had free from the Monster. I am a Survivor...I am a Miracle.

My name is Brendan Burns and I have been a cluster sufferer for just about 20 years now.  Today I am here to talk about the research I have come across and the experiences that I have had over the past 6 years treating my headaches with the dissociative hallucinogen, Ketamine Hydrochloride.
First off I figured I would tell you a bit about myself and my situation.  I had my first cluster when I was 17.  I was lucky enough to be correctly diagnosed within the first 4
years and like most people I was prescribed triptan medications. For a while this worked.  Eventually however, the clusters were getting worse and worse and I was needing a constant supply of triptans.  Not long after I had turned 24 a bit of strangeness stepped in--I started having grand mal seizures due to an undiagnosed gluten allergy.  At this time my clusters started getting much worse but now I was also experiencing other headaches that did not always fit the typical cluster headache profile.  In particular many of them would last longer and act more like a migraine, some sort of other TAC or sometimes a severe tension headache.
Eventually I overcame the seizures by changing my diet but the headaches have remained.  I mention all this because although I have been diagnosed with cluster headaches, I also seem to have some other sort of headache that can mimic cluster at times.   Early on it was hard for me to be able to tell the difference but these days I feel I have a handle on what is what a bit better.

LSD and psilocybin in ‘magic mushrooms’ are among drugs getting redesigned
Psilocybin, found in mind-bending mushrooms, is one of the compounds researchers hope can be developed into drugs that are safe enough to be taken at home.

TEXT
Drug developers are designing new psychedelic compounds to
treat depression and other mental-health conditions
but skip the trip.
Mind-bending psychedelics including MDMA (aka “ecstasy”), “magic mushrooms” and LSD are being studied as potential
treatments for depression, post-traumatic stress disorder and addiction
. Dozens of companies and academic laboratories are also making changes to the structure of those drugs, or designing similar compounds, to take advantage of their therapeutic properties without the high.
Human research on the newly developed compounds is just getting started. Some researchers and analysts said the trip itself could be critical to the therapeutic benefit for patients.
Drugs including psilocybin and mescaline aren’t patentable because they occur naturally in mushrooms or plants. Others including LSD and MDMA were created in labs decades ago.
Global revenue from psychedelics
could reach $8 billion by 2027, L.E.K. Consulting estimated.
Psychedelic drugs are tightly regulated.
Some including MDMA impart effects that last for hours and require patient supervision during medical use.
Companies and researchers said creating new compounds with shorter-acting or non-hallucinogenic effects could lead to fewer restrictions, and make them easier for doctors and patients to use.
New drugs could also open their use to people who are currently excluded from psychedelic treatment for safety reasons, including a history of schizophrenia or some heart conditions, they said.
“We have to create medicines that are safe enough that people can take them at home and put them in their medicine cabinet,” said
David Olson, chief innovation officer at Delix Therapeutics, which is developing drugs similar to substances such as LSD and psilocybin but without the trip. The company said it raised some $100 million and plans to move two drugs into human trials next year.

Hi all,
Sorry about the long hiatus, I’m still alive and fighting the daily CCH battle. I figured I’m over due for an update.
ive been using Ketamine since last October w mixed results. When I first started it I was at my wits end dealing w CH.  6-12 attacks a day lasting on average 3 hrs if I didn’t do anything. Mixed results w Oxygen and trex makes my CH worse over time and isn’t really an option when you’re getting 200-300 attacks a month. Vitamin D helped initially but it will only stop my CH if I stay in the 180-250+ range, aka not safe. Batch tried his hardest to tailor the program to no real success.  I had no life and my parents insisted I go see a doc again. I was working w a Neuro who was several states away and he started ketamine once at 50-75mg/ml nasal spray giving me a max of 45ml a month. I had to use so much to get relief and the doc didn’t want to change my script due to my psych hx. So I gave up.  Months later my parents insisted I go to my previous pain doc who had tried months of SPG and occipital nerve blocks to no success. I told him about the 1 study that included CH Pts w CCH and some other HA conditions.  The study did many ketamine infusions then started Pts on nasal.  He decided to give me oral troches and nasal.  I was taking about a gram of ketamine a day total.  After a few days my CH got much better.  The ketamine would abort the attacks and my frequency and intensity dropped dramatically. Woo hoo I had a life again. Doc was not happy how much ketamine I was on and didn’t realize this was going to be my long term plan so he started to back down the ketamine and switched me to just oral.  I’m currently in 600mg of ketamine a day and my CH is better but my QOL still sucks.  Wake up all night w alarm clock CHs and it takes 2 (100mg troches) to stop a CH plus minus oxygen for the 8-10s.   My attacks last 15-45 min now instead of 3hrs. But I’m still getting 4-8 attacks a day.  If I use my ketamine too fast they come back w a vengeance in 3-5 days. I’ve asked to switch back to nasal only being that it is more effective but he is concerned about my psych hx despite my psych saying he doesn’t think the ketamine was the cause of a hospitalization but was my PTSD.
Im about to move to FL and will be without a doc for the foreseeable future and am concerned about not having ketamine or oxygen. On one hand the ketamine makes life bearable (may be an understatement considering how bad my CH was before) but it’s not stopping them all together as it did in the beginning at higher doses. The other thing is it’s very expensive $450/ month for 180troches. Still less expensive than trex injections but when you don’t have income and have to get help from your parents (who are not wealthy and in fixed income) eventually I’ll have to give it up unless I get disability.
in summation at higher doses 1g a day ketamine almost stopped my attacks dead n their tracks. But when I backed down to about half that dose I get hit 4-6 times a day kipp

So I am going on a trip for a week and the beast starts to rear his ugly head. I do not have time to bust and wonÂ’t be able to while I am gone, but I figure the cycle will ramp slowly and O2 should take care of it till I get back. Well I was an idiot and upon arriving could not resist going to my old brew pub hangout for one of their hand crafted wonders. Well, I get about two hours and slam! So I wonÂ’t be able to bust for more than 5 days anyway so I popped some trex and tried to get some sleep. The next day I was in full high cycle. I know the beer was a bad idea, but I have never known alcohol to effect a cycle. Maybe the beast had just planned to show up full force that quickly, or maybe it was the trex. I do remember someone asking if trex could un-bust youÂ… that was certainly no proof, but damn I hate that stuff. I got through the week with O2 and ketamine, but had to leave early because I was blowing through too much O2. (my E tanks are $15, an M is only $25) Moral of that story is I guess after 38 years of this I am still capable of doing stupid things.
Oxygen Administration: When I first got the optimask I switched to using just the mouth piece. I was happy to finally have all the O2 I wanted (bigger bag). While out of town I noticed I was blowing through more O2 than usual and getting slow results. I remembered how I used to think the negative pressure created in my sinuses by using the mask and sucking through both nose and mouth seemed to Â“pullÂ” the pain out. Even the gentle tug created by the bag running dry was soothing. When I got home I put on the mask and found that the resistance or Â“pullÂ” was greater with the optimask and it felt perfect, however the pressure from the resistance when exhaling through the mask was to greatÂ… going in the wrong direction. I think this is why I chose the mouth piece when I first got it. I began using the mask to inhale, removing it to exhale. The result was faster aborts with less O2. This may be a personal thing, but it made a difference to me. I got to have that soothing sinus cavity suckage!
So I get home and bust immediately. About two hours in slam! I quickly take a dose of the ketamine, crank up the O2 and WhooopsÂ… the cheese slid right off the cracker! I was tripping hard. I was able to keep the beast at bay with the K and O2, but it was a long psychedelic trip. Over the next 5 days the cycle was diminished by about 80%, and the beast was vanquished on the next bust. I guess the point of this part of the story is Ketamine will greatly enhance the effects of mushrooms. If you are uncomfortable with Â“trippingÂ” then you definitely want to stay away from this combination.
I havenÂ’t wanted to tell too much of my story because I donÂ’t want to scare the noobs into thinking this is what they have to look forward to. But I think we all know how unpredictable and terrible this disease can be.

Hello everyone, I am sorry I haven’t responded until now but I do want to answer all of your questions. First, let me tell you a little more background about me. I have a degree in Nursing and worked as a Trauma/ER nurse and a Cardiac Catheterization Nurse for over 20 years. When Cluster Headaches came into my life I was a healthy adult with no prior history of any neurological disorders. I was diagnosed and went through all of the testing one would expect, MRI, Labwork etc. everything came back normal. I immediately started trying to find a “cure” for what was the worst pain and subsequent ancillary symptoms, I had ever experienced. I was familiar with the medical system and had many doctors try and help me. I have tried and had some short relief with therapies such as oxygen therapy, medication, Vitamin D therapy and micro-dosing Magic Mushrooms, high dose Botox injections, Lidocaine injections, Hot pepper nasal spray to name most of them. The problem that continued to occur was that I would get some short term relief, only to see the therapy slowly stop working and would go back into a full cycle.
i have had cycles that lasted 7 months and would have 4-8 severe attacks daily with many ancillary symptoms. I was literally barely surviving each horrific day. I was actively suicidal and honestly believed that I would eventually have to end my life to find freedom from the torment.
I found Ketamine and the clinic because I never stopped looking for something to help stop the attacks. I came across an article about Ketamine treatments for Migraines and they mentioned CH in the article as a possible subset that Ketamine could treat.  The Doctor I see has had over 15 years working with Ketamine and treating Depression and  all forms of headache disorders. We often discuss his observations and let me tell you, what he has seen bears shouting from the rooftops. He said to me during my last booster infusion, that he has seen better results treating CH than he does treating Migraines and his observations estimates about a 70-80% effective treatment with remission and with many with long term remissions at that.
As a Nurse, I have looked at CH as both a horrible and completely debilitating diagnosis for me, but also, with the eyes of a medical professional. I have tried to understand what could be an answer as to why this disorder is so under treated and resistant to so many therapies. I believe in my heart that I am not unique and that Ketamine could be a first line therapy that actually has a significant chance at putting many CH sufferers into long term remission. I decided to post about my experience because I have not seen much online or on any medical journals about Ketamine for CH and I cannot understand why this seems like a hidden treatment or a “last line” treatment. It should not be a treatment for those a the end of their rope, as I was. We all know why this is and if you have experienced any of what I have experienced as a patient seeking help

Of course an intrigue is whether the holotropic breathwork that purportedly induces full on psychedelic states could also possibly serve as some sort of busting technique?
Breathing Their Way to an Altered State
As psychedelics move from the underground to mainstream medicine, clinicians aspiring to work in the field are inducing altered states with deep breathing.
By
Ernesto Londoño
Reporting from San Francisco
Published
Jan. 9, 2024
Updated
Jan. 10, 2024
The instructions were simple: Lying on cots while wearing eyeshades, participants were directed to take deep belly breaths without pause to the beat of fast-paced music booming from loudspeakers.
The exercise, they were told, had the potential to induce an altered state of consciousness so profound that breathers sometimes describe it as reliving the terrifying moment of their birth. Past participants claim to have caught glimpses of past lives.
A few minutes into the session, which lasted nearly three hours, several participants began to weep. Some shook their limbs wildly, looking possessed. An outsider walking in would have been startled by the scene.
But the dozens of attendees at the recent breathwork workshop in San Francisco were far from hippies or cult initiates. They were health care professionals completing the final step of a certificate program in psychedelic therapy.
The vigorous modality, known as holotropic breathwork, is offered at the end of an eight-month training to provide a lawful taste of the therapeutic potential and pitfalls of altered states of consciousness.
Dr. JJ Pursell, a naturopathic doctor from Oregon, was among the trainees who walked into the early October session skeptical that a couple of hours of intense breathing could induce anything close to a psychedelic trip. But she was stunned.
“The depth of what I experienced was so similar to psilocybin,” Dr. Pursell marveled, referring to the psychoactive compound in magic mushrooms. “It was trippy.”
Image
Students in a San Francisco breathwork seminar wept and shook their limbs. “The depth of what I experienced was so similar to psilocybin,” Dr. JJ Pursell recalled.
Credit...
Damien Maloney for The New York Times
A Promising Field of Study, Long Fallow
The demand for psychedelics as tools for healing has exploded in recent years, fueled by promising clinical studies, loosening drug laws and a growing number of celebrities who have come out as psychonauts. The N.F.L. star Aaron Rodgers described ayahuasca ceremonies as a catalyst for “
deeper self love
.” In her new autobiography, the actor
Jada Pinkett Smith
credited ayahuasca sessions with putting an end to her suicidal ideation. And ketamine, an anesthetic that induces a dissociative, psychedelic-like state, has become an
increasingly popular treatment
for depression among people who get it at clinics or self-medicate.

Hi to all.. a quick disclaimer: THIS IS LONG and I apologize. I've considered posting for years, but I'm so very much not inclined to "put myself out there" - maybe a bit of a technophobe.. but i've never chatted, instant messaged, facebooked, etc.Â  I have looked at the well known clusterheadache sites for years, found cluster busters a few years back,Â  and finally decided it's time to share.Â  I think one of the most frustrating things for me as a cluster headache sufferer is the fact that so few people have ever even heard of them, and every time i try to explain to people why i'm clutching my eye, or "am on the wagon" or have oxygen cylinders all over my house, i get the feeling that people just think i need to go get my little tin foil hat and put it on.. if you know what i mean....Â  I want to say to the "community" here that it is very inspiring the way everyone supports one another, and over the years just reading the posts has been so helpful to make me feel like i'm not alone. Others who understand how much this disease can rob your joy.Â  Â  So, thanks to all that have gone before me. I'm glad I found you and that folks were willing to put in the work to create this resource.
Background on me -Â  I'm a male 41 who first began having clusters around 19 or 20 yrs of age. Episodic sufferer now for last 15 years. Usually have one cycle a year, lasting 2-3 months.Â  They started randomly. Would get individual attacks - kip 3-6 with no real pattern. I wonder now if what kept the beast at bay for those first couple of years was the fact that on a few occasions in college (literally 3-4 times) i did small recreational doses of shrooms.Â  I was never a big fan of the idea of "seeing something that wasn't there" so i was very leary.. err.. paranoid of the whole hallucinogen thing - just wasn't my "thing".Â  I liked that i could try shrooms in small doses but when i tried them I always did enough to "feel" it but nothing too intense.Â  When i first read about busting, i was happy to read that you can have success with smaller doses than what is considered "recreational".Â  Â So, back to history..Â  I was around 25 when i had my first real cycle.Â  After about a week of 3 to 5 hits a day of what i used to call "my eye headaches" I finally knuckled under and headed for the family doc... convinced of my upcoming brain scan and diagnosis of brain tumor, aneurism, etc.Â  Doc listened to my symptoms and casually said, 'oh, you have cluster headaches'. (no scans ordered) i was relieved to find out the "what", but it would be several years before i found any real relief.Â  My doc prescribed prednisone to "break the cycle" but never really worked for me.. Each year, I would wait until the cycle became too painful to deal withÂ  and then get my prednisone, would take it, be fine while on it, then the beast would come back just like before.Â  For years i could set my watch by my attacks (between Sept - Dec) usually lasting 10-12 weeks. Hi forum, very glad to find this website!
After reading the various stories, I feel like many were like my own situation. So here is my story...very long and not especially extraordinary, but I thought to include all details in case it helps any other newbie someday (e.g. hey that is just like me). So sorry for the length of post.
I started experiencing clusters when I was 18yr in my first yr university, in spring (March-April) several times a day lasting for 4-6weeks. Unbelievable pain (always right side, started with shadows as I now know them to be called, then jaw and neck pain, then spreading to eye socket, felt like a tapered wooden peg, the kind to kill a vampire with,  being hammered in to the eye socket and out through back of skull, very bad), I took OTC pain meds (tylenol overlap with ibuprofen) but did nothing. Saw family doc, thought it was allergies, sent me to dentist but nothing there, sent me to optometrist (since I mentioned eye pain) and got crappy very low prescription. By that time, the cluster episode was over.
Then I did not have another for a few years, then came back again with same pattern and same thing (tried OTC paid meds, saw family doc, blah blah, run-around until cluster over. text: I was first diagnosed with CH almost 10 years ago by my PCP. She then sent me to a Neuro that actually asked me what kind of medications that I wanted to try and what doses. I did not go back to her. I was then sent to two other neuros that really did not seem to know very much about headaches. I was then sent to one of the best at Mass General, Dr David Biondi. He at first went with the other Drs diagnosis until he tries many types of medications. He then told me that I had CPH. After trying many more medications with horrible side effects he then told me that I had CPH/TN. He sent me to a great Dr to get a nerve block done. This was Dr Julian Vaisman. After I had three nerve blocks done over a couple of years that did help just a little he decided not to do any more and he then told me that I had CPH/TIC. I was having many TICs through the night and some during the day. It was like mini seizures. During my treatment there Dr Biondi spoke of an implant that was being done over seas that might help me but they could not do it here. He even was trying to get me to go over seas to get it done but There was no way taht I could afford it. He even had the Drs that were doing it come over here and show how it was being done at one point. At this point they had closed down the headache clinic at Mass General and Dr Biondi went to work for a private company. I then went to U-mass University to see a Dr there. I got to see and intern but was overseen by a great Dr. Dr Paula Ravin. She was a Dr with NIH for many years and specialized in headaches there. After seeing Dr Ravin she changed my diagnosis to SUNCT/CDH. She told me that my type of headache was idiopathic and atypical so they really do not know exactly what causes it or anything that will treat it at this time. Both Dr Biondi and Dr Ravin told me that I had one of the worst cases that they had treated. That is why Dr Ravin wanted me case. Because I was on State Medicare at this time I had to go see one of their Drs to make sure I was not crazy. I went to see him and he happened to also work for Dr Vaisman. I spoke to this Dr about the surgery that they were doing over seas and he in turn told me that Dr Vaismans office was now doing this surgery on an experimental basis. He also told me that he was the Dr that had to approve each patient for this treatment and he was going to approve me that day. I then went to see Dr Vaisman again. He set me up for the trial implant. He and Dr. Joe Ordia put it in. I got it put in and it worked great for the 10 days. They then removed it and I went back to being bedridden. The insurance company that had approved the trial implant now decided that they were not going to pay for the implant to be put in. My wife then went on the warpath. She called every Senator, congressman, and even the President. She told the insurance company that they could either pay for the implant or she would take me to the ER every day for the rest of my life and they could pay for that.

Hi all, I am writing because I am feeling extremely frustrated after 1 year of mostly consistent MM busting with little payoff. To be clear: I have chronic intractable migraine not cluster headache and have had it chronic intractable for 5.5 years now.
Please buckle up because this is going to be a long post but I am desperate for any suggestions!
The only payoff is the MM has helped with my levels of pain. I will also note I get "Slapback" headaches the next day, despite beig intractable, as the next day my pain is much worse. However at the same time I started busting about 2 months after I had surgery (liposuction) and that surgery increased my baseline pain levels, and I had surgery 3 years before (deviated septum) where my pain increased but went back to baseline after some months. So I’m not even sure if the MM is really doing anything to help with my pain levels or if I would have gone back to the baseline presurgery.
I do not receive medical care in the US, I am based in Spain and our care is much much more heavily restricted than the US. Neurologists are not allowed to do off label prescriptions here. So while I appreciate suggestions of new pills to try, I literally cannot ask for anything new. My current medication list is in the comment section.
We are waiting for Vyepti to become available in Spain. Even then I could be declined so my hopes aren’t up. My neurologist does not know I bust and she cannot know. Only treatments approved by the FDA of Europe are public doctors allowed to recommend.
I see a private psychiatrist and he is aware of my busting. This is because he recommended I take MM on a microdose for my PTSD and depression. When I told him I was busting, he said that it would still be effective for my mental health. I have noticed it has helped my treatment resistant depression. I know some people have used other p$sychd3l!c substances for busting, and I am open to that, if you recommend any, I would have to run it by my psychiatrist because I think he would still recommend I microdose MM because it’s been very effective for my mental health. Also, I’m not sure if microdosing would interact with other p$sychd3l!c busting, making it less effective or not effective at all.
My biggest grievance Is the” side effect” which is being high. I’m kind of at my wits end with it. Yeah its nice on the rare occasion I can go to the beach and take it. But most of the time I’m in bed and watching some movie. I’m sick having to do this so frequently on a weekly basis.
I’ve seen some people say they go to sleep after taking it and I have no idea how. I’ve tried doing this. It worked a few times the last few times it doesn’t work. I take it a few hours before bed and then try to sleep the rest off. I take Klonopin for sleep but it doesn’t matter. text:
So last month, July 6th at 1:50am, I woke up instantly with severe pain in my eye/eye socket, left side all of my face. (almost all symptoms going forward will be on the left side). After a couple minutes of groaning in pain, it became full on sobbing in hysterics. I've had shingles. I have migraines every 2 years on/off. And I'd rather deal with either of them again than this pain.
it lasted for maybe 30mins to an hour, but we did head to the ER at around 2:30am or so. While the Dr there suggested giving my oxygen, the IV with Benadryl helped quelled the pain that was left, as the episode started dying down by the time we got there. That night, it was eye, eye socket, back of my head for pain.
I avoided an episode the next night, possibly because of the meds from the ER. But for about three weeks following, I had attacks at different times.
My boyfriend looked into things that could help, so we got Benadryl in pill form. I took two before sleep and took two every time I woke up during the night. The times I wake up are varied, I have no alarm, I've just always been unable to sleep a full 8hrs uninterrupted. Now this became more effective in stopping a more 'immediate' attack, as for most of those 3 weeks, I ended up dealing with 1-2 attacks a night anyway. But some times it wasn't enough, in that I took some pills but while trying to get back to sleep, dealt with an attack shortly after. At least two attacks have led to me puking for a while during the episode.
So far, Benadryl before sleep or compressing the fuck out of my left temple during an attack were the only things that helped, but they 'evolved' and got worse. Eventually the pain got into teeth, my cheek bone, inside my throat and on my neck.
Every time an attack happens, it starts with a small pain in my temple and while that pain persists, it goes into my eye/eye socket or vice versa and becomes full blown following that. I actually got a teething ring for something to bite to help put pressure on my teeth as well to try and subside it a little.
Now that I've had no attacks since 26th, I'm not 'fine'. My temple will still randomly hurt, or my eye will. I'm also experiencing random swings of nausea and this is happening every day in varying intensity and combination. I've already lost a handful of pounds because I mostly don't want to eat due to the feeling. This has also fucked my sense of temperature. My house is always 79F, not the coldest but not not at all, and there are times where I NEED an icepack or I feel like I'm on fire. I used to be able to sleep with a fan on my face and a foot out from under the sheet and feel a-okay, but it's a toss up if that changes.
I finally saw a neurologist today but he was underwhelming, rude, dismissive and uncaring of the current/lingering problems.
I don't experience during the attacks:  Eye drooping, eye tearing, sweating on one side or facial discoloration. And for those reasons he thinks it could be chronic migraines instead.

Hi everyone!
I've had cluster headaches for over a decade now and have finally gotten them to a place where they're manageable. Here's my story!
I first started getting cluster headaches when I was in 3rd grade, around the same time I started puberty, which I know is young. For a while doctors told me they were due to stress, TMJ, or that they were psychosomatic. I asked my mother if I could see a neurologist but she didn't believe the pain was severe enough to warrant it - which was incredibly demoralizing to me as a little kid.
Fast forward to high school. My headaches had been getting worse and worse and I was getting them more frequently as well. I was deeply suicidal and depressed. I begged my parents to send me to a psychiatrist. Finally, they did. The first one I saw had me on a bunch of different pills, none of which helped. He diagnosed me with ADHD and generalized anxiety disorder. My mom grew frustrated with him and took me to see another psychiatrist, who this time diagnosed me with bipolar disorder. He put me on lithium - it didn't really help with my sadness, but it helped with my headaches very rapidly!
I was overjoyed. I did some googling - at the time I thought I might have migraines - and discovered that lithium is effective for cluster headaches, not migraines. I brought this info to my psychiatrist, asking if he could refer me to a neurologist. He said my headaches were psychosomatic and that I should just calm down. After a few months of being on lithium, my thyroid started to be affected. He took me off lithium and switched me to lamictal. After the switch, I had the worst headache I had ever had in my life. I told my mom to drive me to the emergency room or else I was going to kill myself, the pain was so intense. They put me into psychiatric inpatient and titrated me back up on lithium.
I continued on lithium for a few more years, taking meds to help w the side effects. After I went to college, I started dating someone who wanted to try LSD with me. I figured out I should stop the lithium before doing this, so I went off it, and took a pretty high dose of LSD. After the LSD, I stayed off lithium for a year and had no headaches during that time. I had moved from the northeast to san francisco, so I thought the cause might be the more temperate climate - cold had always triggered my headaches before. Unfortunately, I started getting headaches once more. I did some more research, discovered that psychedelics were often a treatment for CH!
I made an appointment to see a neurologist, both to talk with her about the LSD and get a prescription for oxygen. At the time, I still believed I was bipolar, so I was concerned about taking a psychedelic. Now I'm convinced my mental anguish was from being in an extreme amount of pain and not being believed. I have experience with psychedelics from decades ago, but have not used them (or any recreational drugs, except infrequent cannabis) since the early 90s. I expected very little, but many friends were touting the effectiveness of psychs, and I was at my wits end. On the morning after the fourth microdose, the depression was gone. In almost twenty years, my wife has seen me cry only a few times, and this was one of them. I have had eight just like it since then. I stopped the microdose cycle, but the headaches remain. Advil mitigates the pain after 60-90 minutes, but that is an agonizingly long time, as I am sure everyone reading this knows. I have some oxy left over from a surgery a few years ago, but I am loathe to use it, narcotics make me nauseous, and it likely would not be faster than Advil anyway. I have some ketamine stashed away also, and after seeing that some clinics are using it to treat migraines, I tried some. Someone recommended cannabis, so I loaded my three best vapes with three different strains and kept them by the bedside. text:
After reading, reading, reading on both this site and CH.com for the past few weeks and thinking back on my history with CH, I have had an amazing revelation!
Shortly after the Tet offensive in 1968 ( I was stationed in Chu Lai with the 1st Marines ), I started having what I thought were severe toothaches that radiated across my upper jawbone and into my eyesocket on the right side of my head.  Dental care was almost non existant in South Viet Nam unless you had a broken tooth or were in dire need of an extraction so I just muddled thru them the best that I could and they just went away shortly before I rotated back to the US for discharge in June of that year.
Fast forward to late 1984 - early 1985 when I began experiencing the same type of pain again. Since I knew that I had some cavities in my right upper jaw I went to the dentist after several weeks and had them all fixed and quit having the pain so thought that was it.
The pain came back in 1986 so I went back to the dentist thinking he'd missed something but xrays said no.  Again, I just muddled thru the pain and a few weeks later it went away.
It came back again in late 1987 at which time I made my first trip to the VA hospital in Dallas. After countless visits I was diagnosed with Atypical migraine headaches and the treatment began with one drug after another ( the ones we're all familiar with ) but of course, nothing seemed to work.
By 1989, at the end of my wits and with no health insurance coverage, I found my way to a leading neurologist here in Dallas ( $600 for my initial office visit! ) and was diagnosed with CH.  He gave me Imitrex injections and put me on Varapamil building up to a 480mg per day dose.
Found out about O2 in 2005 or 2006 on the CH.com site during my last cycle before the one I'm in now.
So, to bring you up to date, I was in an almost 3 year remission when this cycle started about 10 weeks ago. I've now been PF for over 48 hours and think maybe the beast has been beaten into submission one more time.  Yeeee Hawww!!
Oh yeah, now to get to my point!  It's about that period between 1968 and 1984 that I had nothing to write about.
You see, shortly after returning stateside in 68 I did what many did; dropped out, tuned in, and turned on.  So, in my best estimate, between mid 1970 and late 1983 there was never an interval greater than 2 to 3 months that I didn't ingest LSD or Shrooms.  Due to some druglife violence that took the life of my best friend and that of my then wife I walked away from that lifestyle and have since remained drug free other that the occasional toke.
So, did I unintentionally beat the beast for that extended period of time?  After all the reading I've done I'm inclined to think so and at 62 years of age I can assure you that come springtime I will be back in the fields that I picked so many years ago in search of "Teonacatl" for my next cycle!!
PFDAN's to all
Dallas Denny

An update.
An interesting night. I had a couple of attacks in the evening and I was awakened few times in the night.  Each time the pain seemed to come from a different place, right side of forehead, then upper temple, then lower temple, the around the jaw and teeth.  That was a new one.  First thought was it was possible that I may have been grinding my teeth but it was not muscular at all.  I was doing some feeling around and at one point I found a very tender spot between the muscles close to the hinge area which I assume was irritation of the nerve in the area, as it seems to radiate along the lower jaw and the area above and along the upper teeth.  The last one was more classic, inflammation of the sinus and it emanated from the area closer to where I consider "normal".
Yesterday I bounced the supplement list  in the provided link against what I typically take daily.  There were a few outliers, omega 3, boron, K2 and the dosage of D3.  I placed an order to fill in the blanks and will look for some alternate Multi-vitamin products.  That said, I do have an anti inflammation regiment I have followed for years centered around Bromelain.  I have had great experiences with this as an anti-inflammatory and anti infection weapon (and digestive and joint pain reduction).
I believe that living in Italy is the reason that as a whole my CH has been progressively becoming less of an issue.  As I mentioned, my last episode was three years ago (in January), the previous was two years before (in March).  Before that (in the Seattle area) it was pretty much random and at least twice a year.  I attribute this to the food and general environment.  Whereas in the states the whole "farm to table" thing is popular, here it is just part life.  Almost all of the food available at the market is grown within a few miles from here.  Every time you go to the market the products are different based on what is in season and nobody expects to have whatever they want whenever they want it.  The farmers I know are very proud of their products and are quite happy the share that they use no chemicals.  Same goes for meat products.  From a health perspective the olive oil is probably the top.  Every year we help a local family to pick olives and are treated to several liters of fresh, unfiltered oil.  Same premise applies to meats.  The chickens here look nothing like the what you buy in the states. Every family make their own sausages in a process they call "pista" which we have participated in several times in which a fresh pig is brought in and processed.  There is always the guy who brings the magic bag of spices (top secret).  And then there is the wine, and most families have a still in the basement.
Sorry to get a bit off track but I needed to throw a little more positive in there to balance things out for my own sanity.  Thought about deleting it but...

text:
Having only a few experiences as a teen 50 years ago, only the first two being vaguely pleasurable, I’ve been trepidatious about a shroom bust. Purchased shroom several times over the last 10 years and always ended up throwing them in the trash. Tried lots of alternative stuff in the interim, including a disastrous horror-show experience with a well-known doctor who gave me whopping dose of ketamine that absolutely incapacitated me for the better part of 18 hrs. Tried micro-dosing shrooms with no appreciable relief.
Finally decided to do it right. Went for acupuncture to chill me out in the morning, made the tea with finely ground powdered shrooms 1.5 grams, lemon and peppermint tea, as per the classic recipe. Carefully filtered out all the shroom sludge. Took a bunch of papaya enzymes beforehand.  Had a good buddy to hang with me just in case. Started out weird but bearable, but got way too intense, what with the visual and auditory Doppler effect on everything. Downed more enzymes and a bunch of different antacids in the vain hope of relieving the nausea. The nausea clearly heightened the general anxiety. All my soothing music CDs became archly overwhelming, tho Louis Armstrong was kind of  grounding LOL! And then there was the constant yawning, a side effect I’d never heard of which didn’t entirely go away for about 48 hrs. Had the typical abstract realizations and insights one kind of expects on shrooms. Told my buddy I needed distraction that was down to earth and simple minded, and made the great (as in cosmically funny) mistake of streaming Woody Allen’s Love and Death which is packed with hysterical dialog crammed with text from Hegel, Goethe, Kandinsky, Socrates, Descartes, Kant etc (ie: the polar opposite of what I wanted) which had me giggling for an hour and half.
When the nausea and the heaviest effect finally wore off, played guitar along with some favorite African recordings and had that great sense of peace, and kind, in a good way, walking around starring in my own movie.
Was headache free for about 36 hrs.
I have this sneaking suspicion (theory?) that had I not had the 5 hrs of nausea - I’d have been able to relax enough to really let go and have a properly productive bust. As it was, I spent the entire experience struggling to stay above the whole thing, because every time I closed my eyes I rabbit holed down into a whooshing torrent of MC Escher - HR Geiger animations, not unlike what it feels to pass out when you’re in shock after a bad accident, which to put not too fine a point on it, feels like you’re dying.
I’d love feedback. Suggestions.

One should be able to get them down to 5-6 pills per day which isn't too much of a task, once you get used to it.
Hopefully Dan is able to come back and give us an update, after having finished the loading dose and moved to maintenance, with his level having elevated into the target range of 80-100ng/ml / 200-250nmol/L.
In respect of the full monty, having ventured down this road myself once before having fell from remission after 7 great pain-free years - it can be overwhelming, particularly elevating your level higher than 80-100ng/ml. I believe for most people the target level for the regimen reflects a vitamin D tank "half full". My lab tests here in New Zealand report the level and state on the report that the risk for hypercalcemia at levels below 200ng/ml are rare. Still, that year another 600,000iu on top of a baseline level of 99.3ng/ml sure had me a little apprehensive, even with my doctors support, albeit I found remission again on day 4 loading at 100,000iu per day and my level elevated close to 180ng/ml that year.
My perspective on non-response to the regimen or falling from remission whilst on the regimen continues to evolve. Research in the migraine field increasingly points to microbial dysbiosis as a significant factor in migraine pathophysiology, given the recent CH studies identifying elevated inflammatory markers in CH patients, I suspect these migraine findings are also relevant to CH. When considered alongside the small but promising case study by Lorenzo and colleagues on the ketogenic diet in chronic CH patients, the full monty may indeed represent a groundbreaking adjunct to the regimen - not only for its antihistamine effects and role in VDR upregulation but also for its potential to target underlying gut-driven inflammation.
There are some clinical case studies that have parallels with the vitamin D3 regimen & full monty in terms of using slightly higher doses of vitamin D3 alongside similar co-factors including herbals such as curcumin and oregano oil in the treatment of other inflammatory conditions, albeit non-related to CH but again, the underlying shared component between the two case reports I have shared below converges around microbial dysbiosis and the treatment protocol centres around modulation of the microbiome using vitamin D3 and co-factors.

I am one of Dr. Krusz patients who has received IV Ketamine therapy.Â  I am likely included in his success statistics quoted above.Â  I have not discussed my experiences on this board because there are so many questionable therapies that are touted, and I did not wish to seem to be just another kook.Â  Now that the therapy is having some proven successes, I thought that IÂ’d share my experiences with the group.
First, about me.Â  I am a male in my mid 50Â’s and have suffered from cluster headaches beginning in my late 20Â’s.Â  I am also an MD, but of course, I had to diagnose my cluster headaches myself.Â  Oxygen is an effective abortive for me.Â  I have tried ergotamine, verapamil, and lithium.Â  I have also used very short courses of steroids which knocks the headaches out for a few days while I get to therapeutic doses of preventatives.Â  Subcutaneous Imitrex injections of 2 to 3 mg. usually produces complete relief in a few minutes.Â  My clusters tend to be about 19 months apart and last 2 months.Â  Yep, IÂ’m a pretty typical episodic cluster headache person.
I first saw Dr. John Claude Krusz, PhD, MD somewhere around March 2007 at his clinic near Presbyterian hospital in Dallas.Â  He considered cluster headaches to be a medical emergency and made an immediate appointment for me.Â  I was in a severe cluster and was completely out of control.Â  He gave me an IV infusion of steroids and started me on Topamax.Â  For some reason, I had never tried this before.Â  Dr. Krusz really knows his medications.Â  His PhD is in pharmacology.Â  He got me started on a gradually escalating dose of Topomax as he tapered the steroids.Â  I got through the cluster better than previous ones as Topamax seems to work much better for me than lithium and verapamil.Â
The beast returned like clockwork in September 2008.Â  I returned to see Dr. Krusz during the third week of my cluster.Â  When I saw him, he told me that he had been using ketamine intravenously on many migraine headache patients with some success.Â  He stated that although anecdotal, the therapy seemed much more effective on cluster headaches.Â  There were no promises made.Â  He just said that it might possibly help.Â  I agreed to this therapy.Â  He and his nurse started an IV on me and slowly infused the ketamine over 1-2 hours.Â  Yeah, it sort of zonked me out, and my memories of the infusion are somewhat scrambled.Â  The nurse checked on me frequently.Â  After the infusion was over, I had no lingering side effects.Â  My wife drove me home, but I still felt perfectly fine.Â  He gave me a prescription for ketamine nose spray to keep a low level of ketamine in my system especially at night when the beast usually attacks.Â  Here is the note that I sent to him after my visit...
Dr. Krusz,
I wanted to drop you a note and tell you about the results of the IV ketamine therapy on my latest cluster headache attack. Â When I arrived at your office, I was about three weeks into my most recent cluster episode

The time of painfree lasts less for a long time if she used imiject (french imitrex) one or two day before the bust. I think the sumatriptan take the place of the tryptamine.
This is something I have been thinking about for a while...I've always just gone along with the idea that Sumatritptan fights for the same receptors that we bust with so the Sumatriptan can "take hold" of the receptors and make it very hard for your busting agents to work.Â  When I started talking about this with my Neuro (The great Brian McGeeney, everybody say hi to him at theÂ  conference
Â  Â Wish I could attend--but it'll happen one of these years!)Â  He said to me, "but they do not actually hit the same receptors...." and it kinda stunned me.Â  I had not actually looked into what the exact receptors are that Sumatriptan is hitting, and had not actually looked into it much.Â  When I did I found he was right (Mostly
)Â  Sumatriptan defintitely does not hit your 5ht2a receptor, which is no surprise because it does not make you actually trip....Both substances do work on the 5ht2D receptor though.Â  Maybe (a big maybe) the 5ht2D receptor is what it's all about for clusters.Â  Psilocybin hits it in a way that gets your headaches down or gone for months at a time, Imitrex hits it not quite so well, making it so that it will abort an individual cluster but without any long term benefit and also taking over the receptor site making it so that things like Psilocybin just doesn't work that well.Â
In the interest of all possibilities being possible, I had to write that.Â
But I don't think that's what's happening.Â  I think it really is all about the 5ht2a receptors.Â  Imitrex does not hit this receptor, but I am 99% positive that it convinces your brain to produce more serotonin.Â  I can't find any links to support this idea, but I am pretty sure it is true. (and truthfully I haven't looked that hard.)Â  Â
In essence I think that the ability for Imitrex to stop a successful bust isÂ  very comparable to the ability of SSRI's to stop a successful trip.Â  As in, if you are on SSRI's you can take a buttload of hallucinogens like Psilocybin but it just does not seem to do what you want.Â  The endogenous serotonin that the SSRI convinces your brain to make fills the 5ht2a receptor spot in and makes it damn hard for the psilocybin to do it's work.Â  I had a friend on SSRI's that told me he took some ungodly amount of mushrooms in an attempt to get them to work and it did something, but he never was actually really tripping....(and I explained to him that he was really lucky he did not end up with serotonin syndrome, but that's another subject).Â  I think the Imitrex is doing the same thing, convincing your brain to make endogenous serotonin which occupies the 5ht2a receptor site, which makes taking Psilocybin or other busting agents close to worthless.
None of this changes any sort of busting routine or anything, but it makes me think there may be a way to tell if it's working, as in--i

I have been reading a bit trying to wrap my brain around the whole LSH thing.Â  In the interest of not hijacking Moxie's seed recipe post I'm going to resurrect this old post with the generic title of "Seeds"because my thoughts on this are probably going to go all over the place.Â  Here are some thoughts I had that I copied and pasted from Moxie's thread.
"we have no idea if LSA is getting converted into LSH.Â  There are people highly skilled in Chemistry that say this conversion does not make sense, and there are people highly skilled in Chemistry that say it does make sense.Â  Until this damn drug war goes the way of Prohibition and fades into obscurity we will most likely not get enough researchers interested to find out the details.Â  (luckily, it seems like that day is getting closer and closer)
This I can say for sure--I have taken seeds in just water and it knocked me down like not much else has.Â  Reminded me of one time in my youth when I was in that "I'll try damn close to anything once" faze and I took some major tranquilizer.Â  Drooling and damn close to impossible to keep my eyes open.Â  Kinda trippy I guess, but more than anything tranquilized.
Comparing that to the experiences when I have combined it with peppermint tea or alcohol, it seemed like a completely different drug.Â  I was zoned, but not tranquilized.Â  Much trippier.Â  No body exhaustion.Â  Completely different animal.Â  Felt like weak, kinda weird LSD.
I have trouble believing this is just a set and setting kind of thing, or a result of being tired before taking seeds.Â
Another thing I will throw out there.Â  I am not convinced that LSA is what is helping us in the seeds.Â  I have never seen any evidence showing LSA is definitively the helpful busting agent, all I have ever seen is results of studies with seed extract.Â  But the RC seeds contain a good bit more than just LSA--they have Isoergine, Ergometrine, Elymoclavine, Chanoclavine, and Lysergol...Maybe more.Â  I understand completely why Andrew Sewell would put out info saying that LSA in these seeds prevent Clusters.Â  (I doubt that he did not consider the idea that it could be something else) My bet is that he knew a basic truth---you tell researchers and Docs that there is some yet to be identified substance in these seeds that prevents clusters and it does not do it for them.Â  You tell them the LSA in these seeds is preventing clusters and it sounds more legit and you get more docs and researchers willing to go along with patients that want to try this.Â  Kinda like how big Pharma tried to tell us they knew how Imitrex worked (by vasoconstriction) and now they're like "ok, maybe not..."
For the record, I am not saying that I think LSA is NOT the busting agent, just that it has not been proven.Â  For that we need pure, pharmaceutical LSA.Â  What we really need is the full array of alkaloidsÂ  that are found in these seeds in pure pharmaceutical form and test them individually. Until then....We just don't know

New to the forums as I recently self-diagnosed. My first cycle was in my teens(18?) and at the time I thought the HAs were going to kill me. Of course I had no idea what the beast had in store for me.
First a quick run down of my HAs. In cycle I generally have two per day. 3 PM-ish and again around 9 PM.  If I am to wake to a HA, which is rare for me, it generally happens in my Summer cycle. I have been fortunate in that Kip 8+ is very, very rare for me.
From age 19 to 20 I was an LSD dealer paying for college. Shame I didn't keep a HA journal back then.  While using LSD regularly and shrooms occasionally(only available a couple times a year)  IIRC I had a year of no episodes at all. Perhaps it is completely coincidental as I had had just two, maybe three cycles total to that point in my life.
Shortly after my 20th birthday I was arrested(no shock there) for distribution of a controlled substance containing LSD analog.  Thankfully I was out of cycle while sitting in the county jail awaiting adjudication. I hit the CJ on March 5th of 1993 and by May 5th of '93 I was in a IDOC(Illinois Dept. of Corrections) facility. And then came June and my Summer cycle.
Obviously, at this point I still had no idea what was going on.  So, as I got comfortable with my new surroundings I started to look for something that would alleviate the pain/allow me to sleep.  I ran into a guy that could get me something strong to let me sleep. His name was "Weasle"... because, well, he looked like a weasle. He was on a script for elavil that has a strong sedative effect for people not accustomed to the drug.  While I found something that allowed me to sleep through my HAs I had also found a trigger for them. But at least I could sleep through them and generally wake up with what I now know to be shadows.
That winter I didn't have a cycle... in fact didn't cycle again till summer of '95. I did have the occasional CH, but nothing like in cycle frequency. During this period out of cycle I found another trigger - marijuana. Yes, you can get marijuana as easily in jail as on the street. It was a pretty regular occurrence - smoke a "jailhouse joint"(toothpick tiny) with my "cellie", 10 minutes later here comes the beast. 4 hours ago, Supportive family said:
I don’t think he tried prednisone but will check under other names too.
You would probably know if he had tried prednisone or another corticosteroid, because it's a multiday thing where you start at a high dose and the take lower doses over subsequent days (a "taper").  It's a treatment that can only be done once a year because of the associated long-term wellness risks, but it usually gives some relief and sometimes will stop a cycle for people with episodic CH.
4 hours ago, Supportive family said:
any recommendations for pain killers to replace tramadol as he prepares for his next bust?
Tramadol "shouldn't" work, since opioids are typically not effective against CH. If triptans don't work, I don't know what to recommend.  There are some second-level abortives that I suppose could be tried -- octreotide is one I can think of, but I wouldn't have high hopes for that.  Some people here with intractable CH have used ketamine (with a doctor's supervision).  There haven't been many reports about that, but it was considered a blessing by a couple who did report. (You can read more by typing ketamine into the search bar.)  Short of ketamine, nerve blocks have helped some people.  Both of those are more overall preventives than abortives for individual attacks.

Contingency Fee Agreement
When you first hire a disability attorney, whether you are filing for SSDI or SSI, you must sign a fee agreement that allows the SSA to pay your attorney if your claim is approved. The SSA will review the agreement to make sure it meets the fee agreement guidelines. This is to ensure that your attorney receives only what he or she is entitled to.
Fee agreements must be on a contingency basis (meaning the attorney will only be paid if you win your disability claim).
How Much Is the Attorney's Fee?
For Social Security disability lawyers, the fee is limited to 25% of the past-due benefits you are awarded, up to a maximum of $6,000. Note that the attorney will be paid only out of your past-due benefits, or "backpay." If no back-dated benefits are awarded, the attorney will not receive a fee; nor is he or she permitted to ask you for one.
It doesn't usually cost you anything to hire a disability attorney; the fee will be paid out of the disability award you eventually receive. Some attorneys, however, will ask you to pay a nominal amount for costs (see below) at the beginning of your case.
When do you have to pay the attorney's fee? Usually, you don't. The SSA takes the entire lawyer's fee (up to $6,000) from your first disability check (your award of backpay), before the agency sends it to you.
Disability Backpay
Once you are approved for benefits, the SSA will calculate the amount of backpay you are owed. For SSDI, your backpay will include retroactive benefits you are owed from the date you were approved back to the date the SSA determined your disability began (for a maximum of 12 months back from the date of your application).
For SSI, your benefits are calculated from the date you are approved for benefits back to the month after you applied for benefits. (For more information, see our article on disability backpay.)
The maximum the disability attorney can charge is 25% of your backpay for his or her services, up to a maximum of $6,000. For example, if your back-dated benefits are calculated to be $10,000, your attorney will be paid $2,500 and you will receive $7,500. However, an attorney is likely to be able to get you more in backpay by negotiating your disability onset date with the SSA -- something you can't do without a hearing (in an "on-the-record" ALJ decision) if you're not represented by an attorney.
Out-of-Pocket Costs
During the course of representation, a disability attorney usually has to request a claimantÂ’s medical, school, work records, and occasionally medical or psychological examinations; these can be expensive. The client must pay these costs separately from the attorney's fee (of 25% of their backpay). Other costs may include charges for copying and postage. It would be unusual in most instances for these costs to exceed two hundred dollars per case (and, usually, the out-of-pocket costs are lower).
